text: The surgeon makes a single 3.5 cm vertical incision through the umbilical skin and inserts the glove port into the intra-abdominal space, preparing for reduced port robotic rectal surgery.
Following the establishment of pneumoperitoneum, the surgeon inserts an additional single port in the right lower quadrant for wristed robotic instruments and rectal retraction.
The operative view shows the access port setup for reduced port robotic rectal surgery, highlighting the arrangement of ports and instruments in the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon performs dissection of the lymph nodes around the root of the inferior mesenteric artery, followed by high ligation of the artery using a hemlock clip plier through the artery arm port.
During the medial to lateral dissection, the surgeon identifies the gonadal vessels and the ureter, clearly visualizing these structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects the left pericolic gutter, progressing upwards towards the level of the spleen, using robotic instruments visible through the operative field.
During the pelvic stage, the assistant surgeon uses an additional single port to retract the recto-sigmoid cephalad, facilitating exposure of the pelvic structures.
The surgeon replaces two 250mm curved cannulas with two 300mm curved cannulas to facilitate deep pelvic dissection.
The surgeon performs pelvic dissection adhering to the principles of total mesorectum excision and autonomic nerve preservation, carefully navigating around the pelvic structures.
The surgeon performs posterior pelvic dissection along the rectal proper fascia, enveloping the rectum and mesorectum while preserving the inferior hypogastric nerves.
The surgeon carefully dissects the lateral aspect of the mesorectal fascia away from the pelvic plexus, ensuring the preservation of the surrounding structures.
Dense adhesions between the pelvic plexus and mesorectum are identified. The surgeon meticulously dissects and gently separates the attachments between the mesorectal fascia and pelvic plexus.
The surgeon identifies the left middle rectal artery and controls it using monopolar diathermy, ensuring precise coagulation within the deep pelvic dissection area.
The surgeon performs anterolateral dissection of the mesorectum, progressing until reaching the previously dissected area at the lateral wall, where posterolateral dissection had been completed.
The surgeon continues the dissection posterior to the vagina, carefully separating the tissue layers in this region.
The magnified robotic view reveals the correct surgical plane, allowing precise identification and dissection of anatomical structures within the pelvic region.

The surgeon performs distal bowel resection using three 45mm endo-wrist staplers inserted through an additional single port in the right lower quadrant, visibly compressing and transecting the bowel tissue.
The surgeon extracts the tumor specimen through the transumbilical incision. An end-to-end intracorporeal anastomosis is performed using a double stapling technique.
The surgeon places a suction drain into the pelvic cavity through the additional single port site located in the right lower quadrant.
